steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim is that Winnemucca directly addresses the reader AND establishes her authority and credibility. The correct quotation must do both: use direct address (speaking to "the reader") and explicitly assert that she has standing to speak on the subject.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "But how can I describe the scene that followed? Some of you, dear readers, can imagine.": Addresses the reader directly, but only invites imagination — it makes no claim about Winnemucca's own authority or credibility. ✗
  • Choice B — "We do not call him a medicine man because he gives medicine to the sick, as your doctors do.": Demonstrates cultural knowledge but does not address the reader with a direct "dear reader" formula, and makes no explicit assertion of personal authority. ✗
  • Choice C — "Now, my dear reader, there is no word so endearing as the word father, and that is why [my people] call all good people father or mother.": Uses direct address ("my dear reader") and shares a cultural observation, but expresses a sentiment about language — not a claim that Winnemucca herself is credible or qualified to speak. ✗
  • Choice D — "My dear reader, I have not lived in this world for over thirty or forty years for nothing, and I know what I am talking about.": Addresses the reader directly ("My dear reader") AND explicitly asserts personal authority through lived experience ("I know what I am talking about"). ✓
That is the correct answer.
